docs.title: Rahul-Chaube-Skills (RCS) - The AI Skills Ecosystem
docs.text: Rahul-Chaube-Skills (RCS) is a production-grade, comprehensive open-source AI Skills Ecosystem and Learning Platform designed for modern Large Language Models (LLMs), autonomous AI agents, serving optimization runtimes, and distributed ML engineering.

docs.title: 🎯 What This Repository Helps With
docs.text:
RCS bridges the gap between raw LLM APIs and production-grade agentic applications. It provides:

  • Structured Prompts: Standardized layouts (YAML frontmatter, XML tags) that enforce reliability and safety constraints.
  • Agent Cognition Patterns: Operational loops (ReAct, Tree of Thoughts, Planning) for autonomous workflows.
  • Model Servings Guidelines: Optimization setups (vLLM caching, quantization, token budgets) that minimize inference costs.
  • Systemic Engineering Blueprints: Complete designs (GraphRAG, MCP client/servers, sandbox environments) for scalable AI infrastructures.

docs.title: 🧭 Learning Pathways
docs.text:
Transition from basic prompt configurations to scaling distributed training setups:

  • Beginner AI Engineer: Prompt anatomy, XML tags, structured JSON schema outputs, and basic client APIs.
  • Intermediate Agent Developer: ReAct execution loops, local state databases, routing, and LangGraph structures.
  • Advanced Cognitive Architect: GraphRAG, Tree of Thoughts search graphs, context pruning, and memory compactions.
  • Expert Systems Optimizer: Model serving (vLLM, SGLang), PagedAttention memory pools, and distributed weights training.
  • Unified Curriculums: 30-day, 90-day, and 365-day curricula.

Explore detailed integration guidelines for each engine in the Models Directory.

docs.title: 🗃️ Skills Catalog (550+ Skills)
docs.text:
The ecosystem is divided into 10 key categories:

  1. Agentic Cognition: ReAct loops, Tree of Thoughts, self-reflection.
  2. Context Engineering: Prompt compression, token budgeting, episodic memory.
  3. Model Serving: vLLM setups, PagedAttention cache, quantization.
  4. Databases & Retrieval: GraphRAG, hybrid vector search, entity merging.
  5. Protocols & Tools: Model Context Protocol (MCP), function calling, sandboxes.
  6. Training & Tuning: LoRA, QLoRA fine-tuning, GRPO, reward modeling.
  7. Observability & Ops: OpenTelemetry, Prometheus logs, evaluation metrics.
  8. Models Customization: Anthropic XMLs, DeepSeek R1 reasoning tokens.
  9. Multimodal & Vision: Vision OCR, video frames sampling, PCM audio streaming.
  10. Business & Product: PRD drafting, ROI cost metrics, data governance rules.

Browse the full catalog map in the Skills Index.

docs.title: 💻 Local Compilation
docs.text:
Compile this repository into a Material Design site locally:

pip install -r requirements.txt
python -c "
import shutil, os
os.makedirs('docs', exist_ok=True)
shutil.copy('README.md', 'docs/index.md')
shutil.copy('FAQ.md', 'docs/FAQ.md')
shutil.copy('ROADMAP.md', 'docs/ROADMAP.md')
shutil.copy('CONTRIBUTING.md', 'docs/CONTRIBUTING.md')
for f in ['pathways', 'projects', 'skills']:
    if os.path.exists(f'docs/{f}'): shutil.rmtree(f'docs/{f}')
    shutil.copytree(f, f'docs/{f}')
"
mkdocs serve

docs.title: Contributing to Rahul-Chaube-Skills (RCS)
docs.text:
Thank you for your interest in contributing to Rahul-Chaube-Skills (RCS)! This repository aims to provide the most comprehensive, production-ready, and advanced skills library for LLMs, AI agents, and autonomous systems.

We welcome contributions of new skills, improvements to existing documentation, bug fixes in guidelines, and new workflow architectures.

docs.title: Code of Conduct
docs.text: By participating in this project, you agree to abide by our Code of Conduct. Please report any unacceptable behavior to security@rahulchaube.com.

docs.title: 1. Proposing a New Skill
docs.text:
If you have a skill that is not currently represented:

  1. Open an issue using the Skill Proposal Template.
  2. Discuss the target use-case, key principles, and prompt engineering instructions with the maintainers.
  3. Once approved, create a pull request placing your skill under skills/<category>/<skill-name>/SKILL.md.

docs.title: 2. Improving Existing Skills
docs.text:
AI models evolve rapidly. If you find a skill has:

  • Outdated prompts or instructions
  • Edge cases where the model fails to follow instructions
  • Missing model-specific instructions (e.g. Claude Code or Cursor details)

Please submit a Pull Request with surgical edits to the corresponding SKILL.md or markdown document.

docs.title: 3. Fixing Bugs or Guidelines
docs.text: If there are errors in our markdown syntax, broken links, or spelling mistakes, feel free to open a quick PR.

docs.title: Style Guidelines
docs.text: To keep the library structured and clean, please adhere to the following rules:

docs.title: Directory Structure
docs.text:
All skills must be categorized under one of the major directories inside skills/:

  • core-behaviors/
  • engineering/
  • ai-ml/
  • models/
  • business-product/

Each skill directory must contain a SKILL.md file.

docs.title: SKILL.md Format
docs.text:
Each skill file must start with a YAML frontmatter:

---
name: skill-name-slug
description: Clear, action-oriented description of what this skill does.
---

Ensure the markdown follows a logical heading hierarchy (#, ##, ###), and uses callouts or code snippets appropriately.

docs.title: Language and Tone
docs.text:
• Write in clear, concise, and professional English.
  • Use imperative, action-oriented instructions for prompt guidelines (e.g., "State your assumptions explicitly" instead of "It is good if you write down the assumptions").
  • Avoid speculative features or complex language.

docs.title: Pull Request Process
docs.text:
1. Fork the repository and create your branch from main.
  2. Implement your changes surgically (touch only what you must).
  3. Format your markdown files with npx prettier --write ..
  4. Ensure linting passes using npx markdownlint-cli "README.md" "docs/**/*.md" "skills/**/*.md".
  5. Submit your PR and describe the changes and motivations clearly in the template.

docs.title: Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ)
docs.text: Here are answers to the most common questions about Rahul-Chaube-Skills (RCS).

docs.title: What is Rahul-Chaube-Skills (RCS)?
docs.text: RCS is a highly comprehensive, modular library of AI agent and LLM guidelines, prompting frameworks, and specialized skill instruction sets. It helps prevent common coding agent errors, guides critical thinking, and teaches models to follow surgical, simple, and goal-driven execution loops.

docs.title: How does this differ from the original Karpathy guidelines?
docs.text:
The original guidelines were derived from a single social media post, covering a few basic guidelines (Think Before Coding, Simplicity First, Surgical Changes, Goal-Driven Execution) in a single file. RCS takes these foundational concepts and expands them into a fully structured, multi-dimensional project containing:

  • 45 specialized skill guides (ranging from systems engineering, RAG, vector databases to deep reasoning, startups, and specific LLM optimization guidelines).
  • Comprehensive documentation detailing philosophy, agent loops, context-window memory management, and evaluation.
  • SVG diagrams explaining architectural flow.
  • Native integration files for Cursor, Claude Code, Windsurf, and custom agent runtimes.

docs.title: Can I use RCS with Cursor?
docs.text: Yes! RCS provides pre-packaged rules under .cursor/rules/. When you open a project with these files, Cursor's Composer and Agent modes will read and enforce these instructions automatically.

docs.title: Can I use RCS with Claude Code?
docs.text: Yes! You can append our CLAUDE.md to your workspace root. Claude Code will automatically ingest these guidelines at the start of any conversation.

docs.title: How do I use individual skills?
docs.text:
Each skill folder (e.g. skills/ai-ml/rag/SKILL.md) contains a description and instructions. When building an agent workflow or prompting an LLM:

  1. Provide the content of the SKILL.md file as a system prompt or part of the agent's instructions.
  2. In agent frameworks (like LangGraph, CrewAI, or autogen), you can read these markdown files dynamically and insert them into specific agent node contexts.

docs.title: The model is spending too much time thinking. How do I fix this?
docs.text: Ensure you are using the correct rules. Read our guidance on AI Agents and Philosophy. You can add a constraint telling the model: "For simple typo fixes or obvious code additions, bypass the deep thinking planning phases and execute surgically."

docs.title: Can I customize the skills?
docs.text: Absolutely. RCS is built to be customized. You can copy the skill folders you need into your project and customize the SKILL.md instructions to match your team's specific styles, standards, and libraries.

docs.title: Rahul-Chaube-Skills (RCS) Roadmap
docs.text: This roadmap outlines the planned enhancements and future directions for Rahul-Chaube-Skills (RCS).

docs.title: 🎯 Vision
docs.text: To build the most advanced, structured, and modular behavioral framework for LLMs and autonomous AI agents, enabling developers to build systems that think critically, write surgical code, and collaborate effectively.

docs.title: 🗺️ Execution Milestones
docs.text:
timeline
    title RCS Evolution Milestones
    Q3 2026 : Core Release (v1.0.0) : 45 Skills Architecture : Multi-page Docs Portal : Rebranded Cursor & Claude Rules
    Q4 2026 : Integration & Tooling : Auto-Plugin Compiler : SDK for LangGraph & CrewAI : Dynamic Skill Server
    Q1 2027 : Validation & Testing : RCS Benchmark Engine : Multi-Agent Simulator : Custom Skill Generator CLI
    Q2 2027 : Advanced Reasoning : Multi-modal vision-guided execution : Long-term semantic memory patterns : Self-healing agent nodes

docs.title: 1. SDK and Framework Integrations
docs.text:
• Auto-Compiler: Convert any RCS skill folder into standard MCP (Model Context Protocol) configurations, Claude Code plugins, or Cursor rulesets with a CLI tool.
  • LangGraph & CrewAI Adapters: Ready-made prompt utilities to load RCS skill instructions directly into agent nodes in Python and TypeScript.

docs.title: 2. Validation & Evaluation (RCS Benchmarks)
docs.text:
• Agent Evaluation Framework: A suite of test scenarios to evaluate how well a model follows the "Think Before Coding," "Simplicity First," and "Surgical Changes" rules.
  • Scorecards: Automatic grading of model outputs to help developers choose the best LLM backend for their agent workflows.

docs.title: 3. Advanced Multimodal & Spatial Reasoning
docs.text:
• Visual Verification Guidelines: Deepen instructions for models using computer use, vision, and GUI interactions, preventing "misclicks" and endless verification loops.
  • State tracking rules: Formal guidelines for maintaining agent workspace layout maps during visual tasks.

docs.title: 4. Interactive Development CLI
docs.text:
• RCS CLI: A command-line utility to:
  • rcs init – Initialize project rules in a workspace.
  • rcs list – List available skills.
  • rcs add <skill-name> – Download a skill folder into the workspace.
  • rcs check – Validate the workspace files for lint and rule alignment.

docs.title: AI Agents
docs.text: This document guides the design and execution of autonomous AI agents utilizing the Rahul-Chaube-Skills (RCS) library.

docs.title: 🔁 The Agent Loop
docs.text:
An RCS-compliant agent operates in a structured loop to ensure tasks are completed correctly, simply, and with minimal side effects.

graph TD
    UserRequest[User Request] --> PlanPhase[1. Plan: Analyze & Assume]
    PlanPhase --> Review[2. User Review & Approval]
    Review --> Execute[3. Execute: Surgical Coding]
    Execute --> Verify[4. Verify: Run Tests / CLI Checks]
    Verify -- Failure --> Execute
    Verify -- Success --> Walkthrough[5. Walkthrough & Summary]
    Walkthrough --> Done[Task Complete]

docs.title: 🛑 Escaping Thinking Traps
docs.text:
AI agents often get stuck in repetitive loops:

  • The Research Trap: Searching the codebase repeatedly for the same files.
  • The Execution Trap: Trying to fix a failing compilation with speculative edits without reading the compile log.
  • The Validation Trap: Running the same test repeatedly, hoping the failure disappears.

docs.title: Rules to Break Traps:
docs.text:
1. Incremental Escalation: If a terminal command fails twice, stop. Read the error log, write a temporary scratch script to reproduce it in isolation, and fix it there.
  2. Context Reset: If the agent conversation goes beyond 10 turns on a single bug, review the history using conversation logs (transcript.jsonl), summarize the state, and ask the user for advice or a sanity check.
  3. No Speculation: Never write code to fix an error if you do not understand the error message.

docs.title: 🏗️ Agent Design Patterns
docs.text:
Production AI agents should be constructed based on task complexity:

  • Router Pattern: A centralized LLM directs queries to specific subagents or toolsets based on intent classification.
  • Orchestrator-Workers Pattern: An orchestrator splits a complex goal into parallel tasks, delegates them to independent workers, and synthesizes the results.
  • Collaborative Multi-Agent Networks (Chat / Graph): Specialized agents communicate in structured topologies (e.g. state-sharing graphs like LangGraph).

docs.title: 🛠️ Tool Calling Guidelines
docs.text: Tool calling is the primary interaction interface between the agent and its execution environment.

docs.title: 1. Schema Definitions
docs.text:
Define function schemas using strict formats (like JSON Schema or Pydantic):

  • Every argument must have a detailed description explaining boundaries, formatting expectations, and defaults.
  • Leverage typing constraints (e.g. enum for categorical choices, minimum/maximum for numbers).

docs.title: 2. Failure Handling & Recovery
docs.text:
• Parameter Validation: Validate inputs at the client level before forwarding to the host tool execution engine.
  • Error Escalation: If a tool call fails, capture stderr and return a structured JSON response (e.g. {"status": "error", "message": "..."}) to let the model self-correct rather than crashing.

docs.title: 🧠 Memory Systems
docs.text:
Memory allows agents to maintain state and recall facts across long executions.

                  ┌────────────────────────┐
                  │      Agent Memory      │
                  └───────────┬────────────┘
                              │
         ┌────────────────────┴────────────────────┐
         ▼                                         ▼
┌─────────────────┐                       ┌─────────────────┐
│Short-term Memory│                       │Long-term Memory │
│(Sliding Window) │                       │(Vector Database)│
└─────────────────┘                       └─────────────────┘
  • Short-Term Memory (Context Memory): Managed via chat history threads, pruned using context compaction techniques.
  • Episodic Memory: Storing historical trace logs (like transcript.jsonl) to help the model learn from past runs.
  • Semantic Compaction: Programmatically extracting entities, key facts, and decisions made in the conversation and storing them in a structured knowledge base (like a local key-value store or hybrid database).

docs.title: 🖥️ Browser & Computer Use
docs.text: Browser and Computer Use loops allow agents to interact directly with graphical user interfaces (GUIs).

docs.title: 1. Coordinate Navigation
docs.text:
• Screenshot Ingestion: Periodically capture the screen state as high-resolution PNGs and feed them into multimodal vision encoders.
  • Element Mapping: Overlay interactive elements with numeric bounding boxes (interactive grids) to help the model select precise coordinates.

docs.title: 2. Action Verification
docs.text:
• Double-Check State Change: Never assume a click succeeded. Capture a screenshot after the action and compare visual changes (e.g., changes in text input focus or loader animations) to confirm state updates.
  • Wait Buffers: Implement dynamic wait buffers to allow web pages and API integrations to resolve completely before running subsequent inputs.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: This document explains the system design of Rahul-Chaube-Skills (RCS).

docs.title: 🏗️ Design Overview
docs.text:
RCS is structured as a hierarchical prompting system, mapping broad behavioral rules to fine-grained tools and instructions.

graph TD
    A[Core Behavior Guidelines: CLAUDE.md / MDC Rules] --> B[Skill Dispatcher]
    B --> C1[Core Behaviors Category]
    B --> C2[Engineering Category]
    B --> C3[AI & ML Category]
    B --> C4[Model Specific Tuning]
    B --> C5[Business & Product]

    C1 --> D1[reasoning / planning / coding]
    C2 --> D2[frontend / backend / databases]
    C3 --> D3[rag / memory / tool-use]

    D1 -.-> E[LLM Context Window / Execution Runtime]
    D2 -.-> E
    D3 -.-> E

docs.title: 1. Global Behavioral Level
docs.text:
Found at the root of the workspace (CLAUDE.md, .cursor/rules/rcs-behavior-guidelines.mdc). These files serve as the foundation, enforcing:

  • Thinking before taking action
  • Keeping code simple
  • Making minimal surgical changes
  • Testing and verifying every change

docs.title: 2. Category Level
docs.text:
Skills are grouped by application category under skills/:

  • core-behaviors: Universal cognitive templates for coding agents.
  • engineering: Instructions for systems infrastructure, development, testing, and optimization.
  • ai-ml: Patterns for prompt engineering, agent control loops, and API integrations.
  • models: Specific API optimization adjustments to make standard models (with EverestQ as the native model) behave under constraints.
  • business-product: Guides for user experience, startup operations, and product specifications.

docs.title: 3. Skill Level (SKILL.md)
docs.text:
Every leaf node in the skills library contains a SKILL.md file. Each skill has:

  • YAML Frontmatter: For cataloging and programmatic loading.
  • Cognitive Rules: Constraints directing the model's approach to tasks in that domain.
  • Syntactic Templates: Concrete examples and patterns of expected output structure.

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text: This guide outlines best practices for developers using or extending Rahul-Chaube-Skills (RCS) in their production AI pipelines.

docs.title: 1. Let the Agent Plan (Planning Mode)
docs.text:
When using Cursor Composer or Claude Code, encourage the agent to write a plan inside implementation_plan.md before writing code.

  • Avoid instructing the agent to "just write the code."
  • Instead, say: "Create a plan first, then wait for my approval."

docs.title: 2. Force Test-Driven Development (TDD)
docs.text:
When asking for changes, define the success criteria in terms of tests:

  • "Implement feature X. First write a failing unit test, verify it fails, then write the code to make it pass."
  • This forces the agent into a strict verification loop, reducing logic errors.

docs.title: 3. Review Diffs Surgically
docs.text:
When reviewing pull requests generated by an AI agent, verify that:

  • No comments were deleted.
  • No formatting style was altered (e.g. quote styles, indentation).
  • No unrelated functions were refactored.

docs.title: 1. Write Clear Constraints
docs.text:
When defining prompts inside a SKILL.md file, use strict command language.

  • Good: "You must only use relative imports."
  • Bad: "Try to use relative imports if possible."

docs.title: 2. Include Examples
docs.text: LLMs are highly responsive to few-shot learning. Provide a concise block of Before and After code or text representing the skill.

docs.title: 3. Keep Skills Focused
docs.text: Do not create a skill like full-stack-web-development. Instead, split it into frontend, backend, and state-management skills. Focused instructions result in higher model alignment.

docs.title: Coding Guidelines
docs.text: This document details the code-writing and refactoring standards enforced by Rahul-Chaube-Skills (RCS).

docs.title: ✂️ Surgical Changes
docs.text: The most important rule in writing code with AI is to make surgical modifications. This means targeting only the lines that directly resolve the task, leaving adjacent lines completely untouched.

docs.title: Surgical Coding Rules:
docs.text:
1. Match Code Style: If the file uses double quotes ("), use double quotes. If it uses tab indents, use tab indents.
  2. Preserve Comments: Do NOT delete, rephrase, or move comments in adjacent code.
  3. No Drive-By Refactoring: Do not improve clean code or refactor methods that are unrelated to the task. If you see a major code smell, present it as a suggestion in your final walkthrough—do not change it.
  4. Remove Unused Imports Immediately: If your edits render an import, variable, or function unused, delete it. Do not leave dead code behind.

docs.title: 💡 Simplicity First
docs.text:
Over-engineered code is a common LLM pitfall. Avoid these patterns:

  • No Speculative Abstractions: Do not build interfaces or abstract classes for components that only have one implementation.
  • No Speculative Handling: Do not write complex try-catch statements for scenarios that are logically impossible or out of scope.
  • Short Files: If a file exceeds 500 lines, see if it can be broken down or kept simple.

docs.title: Evaluation & Verification
docs.text: This guide outlines verification and evaluation processes within Rahul-Chaube-Skills (RCS).

docs.title: 🧪 Goal-Driven Verification Loops
docs.text: Every code change must be verified against predefined success criteria before the turn is completed.

docs.title: Verification Matrix:
docs.text: Verification Type Method Scope Syntactic Compiler / Linter (e.g. tsc, eslint, markdownlint) Checks syntax errors, typos, and formatting alignment. Functional Unit Tests / Integration Tests (e.g. jest, pytest) Checks code logic against expected input/output behaviors. Systemic End-to-End Tests / API manual tests (curl, browser clicks) Verifies that endpoints and workflows operate in production contexts.

docs.title: 🔄 The Verification Loop
docs.text:
graph TD
    CodeEdit[1. Write Surgical Changes] --> RunChecks[2. Run Compiler / Lints]
    RunChecks -- Compile Error --> FixAST[3. Fix AST / Syntax]
    FixAST --> RunChecks
    RunChecks -- Compile Pass --> RunTests[4. Run Unit Tests]
    RunTests -- Test Fail --> FixLogic[5. Refactor Code Logic]
    FixLogic --> RunChecks
    RunTests -- Test Pass --> ManualVerify[6. Run E2E / Manual Curl Check]
    ManualVerify -- Failure --> FixLogic
    ManualVerify -- Success --> Complete[7. Done: Update Walkthrough]

docs.title: Real-World Examples
docs.text: This document demonstrates the core principles of Rahul-Chaube-Skills (RCS) using concrete code examples. Each section presents a common LLM mistake and shows the correct, RCS-aligned approach.

docs.title: Example 1: Hidden Assumptions
docs.text: User Request: "Add a feature to export transaction logs."

docs.title: ❌ What Agents Do (Wrong Assumptions)
docs.text:
The agent silently assumes details and writes bloated code:

def export_transactions():
    # Silently assumes database format, CSV columns, and file storage path
    logs = Transaction.query.all()
    with open('/tmp/transactions.csv', 'w') as f:
        writer = csv.writer(f)
        for log in logs:
            writer.writerow([log.id, log.amount, log.timestamp])
    return "Exported!"

docs.title: ✅ RCS Alignment (Surface Assumptions First)
docs.text:
The agent stops and requests clarification before editing:

Before implementing, I have identified several assumptions that require clarification:
1. **Scope**: Should we export all transactions, or should we filter by date/user?
2. **Format**: You requested an "export". Did you mean:
   - A downloadable CSV file via a REST API endpoint?
   - A background task that uploads a JSON file to AWS S3 and emails the link?
3. **Fields**: Which transaction columns are required? (e.g. ID, Amount, Timestamp, Status, UserID).
4. **Volume**: If logs exceed 100k rows, we should implement streaming. What is the expected scale?

Simplest approach: Create an endpoint that returns paginated JSON logs.
Please clarify your requirements.

docs.title: Example 1: Premature Abstractions
docs.text: User Request: "Write a function to hash passwords."

docs.title: ❌ What Agents Do (Overengineered)
docs.text:
from abc import ABC, abstractmethod

class PasswordHasher(ABC):
    @abstractmethod
    def hash(self, password: str) -> str: pass

class BcryptHasher(PasswordHasher):
    def hash(self, password: str) -> str:
        return bcrypt.hashpw(password.encode(), bcrypt.gensalt()).decode()

class HasherFactory:
    @staticmethod
    def get_hasher(algorithm: str) -> PasswordHasher:
        if algorithm == "bcrypt": return BcryptHasher()
        raise ValueError("Unsupported algorithm")

docs.title: ✅ RCS Alignment (Keep It Simple)
docs.text:
import bcrypt

def hash_password(password: str) -> str:
    """Hash password using bcrypt."""
    return bcrypt.hashpw(password.encode(), bcrypt.gensalt()).decode()

docs.title: Example 1: Collateral Formatting Changes
docs.text: User Request: "Fix the typo in the error response message."

docs.title: ❌ What Agents Do (Reformatting Adjacent Code)
docs.text:
def process_payment(amount):
-     if amount <= 0:
-         raise ValueError('amount must be positive')
-     # Charge customer card
-     charge = stripe.Charge.create(amount=amount)
-     return charge
+     """Process Stripe payments."""
+     if amount <= 0:
+         raise ValueError("Amount must be greater than zero.")
+
+     # Charge card
+     charge = stripe.Charge.create(
+         amount=amount,
+         currency="usd"
+     )
+     return charge

docs.title: ✅ RCS Alignment (Surgical Edit)
docs.text:
def process_payment(amount):
      if amount <= 0:
-         raise ValueError('amount must be positive')
+         raise ValueError('Amount must be positive')
      # Charge customer card
      charge = stripe.Charge.create(amount=amount)
      return charge

docs.title: Installation & Setup
docs.text: This guide walks you through integrating Rahul-Chaube-Skills (RCS) into your AI development environments.

docs.title: 🚀 Supported Platforms
docs.text:
RCS is a platform-agnostic specification. It can be integrated as:

  1. Cursor MDC Rules (Highly recommended for Editor integration)
  2. Claude Code Behavioral Rules
  3. Windsurf System Guidelines
  4. Agent SDK System Instructions (LangGraph, CrewAI, Autogen, etc.)

docs.title: 1. Cursor Setup (Composer / Agent)
docs.text:
Cursor natively reads custom rulesets defined inside .cursor/rules/.

  1. Copy the rules from this repository: 
    mkdir -p .cursor/rules/
cp -r path-to-rcs/.cursor/rules/* .cursor/rules/
  2. The rulesets (rcs-behavior-guidelines.mdc, rcs-coding.mdc, etc.) will automatically apply to all composer prompts and actions.

docs.title: 2. Claude Code Setup
docs.text:
Claude Code looks at a CLAUDE.md file in the root of your project directory for guidance on formatting, tests, and behavior.

  1. Copy the CLAUDE.md file to the root of your workspace: 
    cp path-to-rcs/CLAUDE.md ./CLAUDE.md
  2. Claude Code will read this file during start-up.

docs.title: 3. Custom AI Agent Integration (Python / Node.js)
docs.text: To load RCS skills dynamically into custom workflows:

docs.title: Python Example (LangGraph / CrewAI)
docs.text:
import os

def load_rcs_skill(category: str, skill_name: str) -> str:
    """Loads a specific RCS skill instructions string."""
    skill_path = os.path.join("skills", category, skill_name, "SKILL.md")
    if not os.path.exists(skill_path):
        raise FileNotFoundError(f"Skill not found: {skill_path}")

    with open(skill_path, "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        content = f.read()

    # Optional: strip YAML frontmatter
    if content.startswith("---"):
        parts = content.split("---", 2)
        if len(parts) >= 3:
            return parts[2].strip()
    return content.strip()

# Load reasoning skill for your Agent Node
reasoning_prompt = load_rcs_skill("core-behaviors", "reasoning")

docs.title: LLM Optimizations
docs.text: Different Large Language Models have distinct behaviors, context limits, and reasoning styles. This guide explains how to optimize Rahul-Chaube-Skills (RCS) rules for specific models.

docs.title: 1. EverestQ
docs.text:
EverestQ is a high-performance model supported by the RCS platform. It excels in complex multi-step reasoning, strict negative constraint adherence, and surgical code modifications.

  • Tuning: Use strict XML tag structures to isolate system configurations. Set the temperature to 0.0 for code-writing and logical reasoning. Optimize KV caching at the serving layer using PagedAttention to maintain rapid throughput.

docs.title: 2. Anthropic Claude (3.5 Sonnet / Opus)
docs.text:
Claude is highly analytical and follows system instructions closely, but can occasionally over-explain.

  • Tuning: Force Claude to be concise. Use XML tags (e.g. <thought> or <plan>) to separate planning from execution. Claude responds well to clear, formal constraints.

docs.title: 2. OpenAI GPT-4o / GPT-4
docs.text:
GPT models are highly efficient for everyday coding, but can sometimes silently ignore negative constraints (e.g. "Do not refactor X").

  • Tuning: Put negative constraints in capital letters or bold them. Repeat key safety instructions in the system prompt.

docs.title: 3. Google Gemini (1.5 Pro / Flash / Gemini 2.0)
docs.text:
Gemini features a massive context window (up to 2M tokens) and excellent multimodal support, but can occasionally lose focus on long tasks (needle-in-a-haystack in prompting).

  • Tuning: Keep task descriptions structured. Do not dump the entire codebase into the prompt just because Gemini can handle it. Limit context size to keep the reasoning speed fast.

docs.title: 4. DeepSeek (V3 / R1)
docs.text:
DeepSeek-R1 utilizes extensive internal Chain-of-Thought (thinking tokens) before responding.

  • Tuning: Let the model think. Do not try to bypass or limit thinking tokens, as they are crucial for DeepSeek's logical reasoning and code correctness.

docs.title: 🧭 Model-Specific Prompt Patterns
docs.text:
Adapting prompts to individual model biases yields significant performance gains:

  • XML Tag Isolation (Claude / EverestQ): Wrap system contexts inside explicit XML block nodes. This allows the parser to split static parameters from user inputs surgically.
  • Capitalization Constraints (OpenAI / Llama): Enforce negative constraints by capitalizing words (e.g. DO NOT overwrite existing docstrings).
  • Chain-of-Thought (CoT) Freedom (DeepSeek R1): Provide clear thinking enclosures (e.g. Output your complete thinking plan before coding) to trigger deep reasoning pathways.

docs.title: 🚀 Production Serving & Deployment Patterns
docs.text: When serving LLMs in production configurations, optimization at the execution layer is critical to maintain reasonable latencies and reduce hosting costs:

docs.title: 1. KV Cache Optimization (PagedAttention)
docs.text:
• Standard attention mechanisms store keys and values (KV cache) in contiguous GPU memory. PagedAttention partitions the KV cache into logical blocks, avoiding fragmentation and enabling a 10x throughput increase in parallel request serving.
  • Enable Prompt Caching to bypass reprocessing static system instructions on subsequent turns.

docs.title: 2. Quantization Profiles
docs.text:
Deploying weights at full precision (FP16/FP32) is prohibitively expensive:

  • AWQ (Activation-aware Weight Quantization): A 4-bit quantization scheme that keeps key activations at higher precisions, retaining model reasoning accuracy while reducing GPU memory footprints by 70%.
  • GPTQ / GGUF: Run quantized architectures locally on edge nodes or CPU setups.

docs.title: ⚠️ Failure Modes & Anti-Patterns
docs.text: Avoid these common mistakes when deploying AI models in agentic systems:

docs.title: 1. Context Overloading (Information Stuffing)
docs.text:
• Anti-pattern: Feeding the entire codebase or historical trace log directly into the context window simply because the model supports a large token limit.
  • Failure Mode: The model loses focus on task constraints (the "needle-in-a-haystack" retrieval drop).
  • Fix: Implement surgical context engineering (AST pruning, sliding windows).

docs.title: 2. Speculative Editing
docs.text:
• Anti-pattern: Emitting updates without compiling or verifying linter warnings locally.
  • Failure Mode: The agent creates syntax errors in adjacent code blocks.
  • Fix: Force pre-flight planning and closed linter-healing loops.

docs.title: Context & Memory Management
docs.text: This document details memory management strategies for AI agents using Rahul-Chaube-Skills (RCS) to maximize context-window efficiency.

docs.title: 💾 Context-Window Conservation
docs.text: As conversation history grows, LLM performance, response latency, and instruction-following quality degrade. Managing context window space is critical.

docs.title: Memory Optimization Rules:
docs.text:
1. Surgical File Views: Never view a file's entire content if you only need to inspect a 20-line method. Use the StartLine and EndLine arguments of view_file.
  2. Compact Command Outputs: Run commands with parameters that limit verbose output (e.g. git log -n 5 instead of git log).
  3. Avoid Repetitive Searches: Cache search results mentally. Do not run the same grep_search or list_dir multiple times in a single session.
  4. Summary Truncation: When referencing past events, reference the conversation transcript transcript.jsonl or summarize findings in a compact paragraph rather than paste pages of conversation logs.

docs.title: 🗄️ Structured Memory Storage
docs.text:
State should be preserved across agents using file-based storage:

  • Task List (task.md): A checklist tracking task execution progress.
  • Implementation Plan (implementation_plan.md): Architectural blueprints and approvals.
  • Walkthrough (walkthrough.md): Historical records of modifications and testing results.

docs.title: Philosophy
docs.text: The core philosophy of Rahul-Chaube-Skills (RCS) is built upon reducing LLM cognitive pitfalls and maximizing agent leverage through four foundational pillars: Thinking, Simplicity, Precision, and Verification.

docs.title: 🧭 The Four Pillars
docs.text:
mindmap
  root((RCS Philosophy))
    Think Before Coding
      State assumptions explicitly
      Identify confusion early
      Avoid premature execution
    Simplicity First
      No speculative features
      Avoid over-abstraction
      Minimize code footprint
    Surgical Changes
      Match existing styles
      Only edit target lines
      Prevent collateral damage
    Goal-Driven Loops
      Define clear success criteria
      Loop until verified
      Write tests first

docs.title: 🧠 1. Think Before Coding
docs.text:
• Pitfall: LLMs often jump into generating files or commands based on the first interpretation that comes to mind, frequently guessing incorrect API schemas or configuration locations.
  • RCS Solution: Stop and reason. State assumptions. Present alternative interpretations. If unsure, stop and ask the user for clarification.

docs.title: 🎯 2. Simplicity First
docs.text:
• Pitfall: LLMs have a tendency to write over-engineered code, adding abstractions, classes, interfaces, and unused configurations in an attempt to be "flexible."
  • RCS Solution: Build the absolute minimum code required to solve the immediate problem. Do not write speculative code for "future features." Keep files short.

docs.title: ✂️ 3. Surgical Changes
docs.text:
• Pitfall: Code editors or terminal agents often overwrite large blocks of files or refactor adjacent methods, introducing side-effects, syntax errors, and breaking changes in code that was orthogonal to the task.
  • RCS Solution: Touch only what you must. Do not clean up unrelated code unless requested. Respect the style and patterns of the surrounding file.

docs.title: 🔄 4. Goal-Driven Execution
docs.text:
• Pitfall: Agents often loop indefinitely or report "done" without ever validating if the code compiles, the endpoints respond, or the tests pass.
  • RCS Solution: Convert instructions into measurable success criteria. Define verification steps before starting, then loop iteratively until the verification succeeds.

docs.title: Prompt Engineering
docs.text: This document details the prompt engineering methodologies implemented within Rahul-Chaube-Skills (RCS) to enforce behavioral alignment on LLMs.

docs.title: 🛠️ Systemic Instruction Patterns
docs.text: RCS leverages three primary prompt design patterns:

docs.title: 1. The Pre-Flight Checklist (Planning Constraint)
docs.text:
Before the model emits any code, we force the generation of a mental model.

  • Instruction Pattern: 
    Before you make changes or execute any terminal command, write down:

1. Assumptions: What are you assuming about the data/API?
2. Confusion: What is ambiguous?
3. Tradeoffs: What are 2-3 alternative approaches?

docs.title: 2. Negative Constraints (Behavioral Guardrails)
docs.text:
Negative constraints (telling the model what not to do) are often more effective at preventing LLM regressions than positive suggestions.

  • Examples:
  • "Do NOT write speculative helper functions."
  • "Do NOT alter formatting in lines adjacent to your changes."
  • "Do NOT delete pre-existing comments."

docs.title: 3. Verification Loop Triggers (Success Boundaries)
docs.text:
We force the model to define its own test cases before starting, preventing it from halting prematurely.

  • Instruction Pattern: 
    State your verification plan before editing:

- Unit Test command: [command]
- Expected Output: [output]
- Manual verification curl: [command]

docs.title: 🔄 Optimizing for System Prompts
docs.text:
When configuring your own agent templates:

  1. Place constraints at the bottom: LLMs tend to pay more attention to instructions placed at the beginning and the end of the prompt context (recency bias). Place behavioral guardrails near the final user prompt.
  2. Use Markdown tags: Use clear enclosures like <guidelines> or <rules> to help the model separate system guidelines from user inputs.

docs.title: 💾 Context Engineering
docs.text: Context Engineering is the practice of optimizing the structure, content, and token allocation of the LLM context window to ensure high-fidelity reasoning and prevent context recall degradation (loss of focus).

docs.title: 1. Token Budget Allocation
docs.text:
Always partition your token budget systematically:

  • System Instructions: 10-15% (static, cached).
  • Core Skill Templates: 20-25% (dynamic, loaded on-demand).
  • Short-Term Memory (History): 30-40% (sliding window, compacted).
  • Current Task Context: 20-30% (files, terminal stdout, user query).

docs.title: 2. Context Pruning & Compaction
docs.text:
When context limits are approached, execute compaction routines:

  • Summarize Old Conversations: Replace historical back-and-forth turns with a high-density markdown summary of state updates.
  • AST Pruning: For code references, replace full files with class definitions, method signatures, and docstrings. Only inject the fully detailed code for the specific file targeted for changes.
  • Sliding Attention Windows: Maintain a sliding window of historical turns, pruning older turns while keeping key facts stored in a structured key-value memory map.

docs.title: 🔁 Verification Loops & Self-Correction Gates
docs.text:
A robust prompt engineering setup must enforce a closed-loop verification cycle, preventing the model from declaring a task complete without programmatic confirmation.

graph TD
    A[Generate Solution] --> B[Self-Review Gate]
    B -->|Self-Correction Needed| A
    B -->|Check Passed| C[Programmatic Compile / Lint]
    C -->|Failure Detected| D[Error Parser]
    D -->|Inject Stacktrace to Context| A
    C -->|Success| E[Task Complete]

docs.title: 1. Self-Reflection Prompts
docs.text:
Direct the model to critique its own solution before emitting:

Verify your output against the following rules:

- Does it contain any speculative imports?
- Did you modify any lines outside the specified range?
  If any violations are found, rewrite the solution.

docs.title: 2. Programmatic Correction Hooks
docs.text:
Set up automated triggers in your agent's execution loop:

  • Linter-Heal Gate: Feed linter outputs (errors, warnings) back into the prompt context, directing the model to correct the specific line numbers.
  • Compiler Checks: Run test suites or compiler checks automatically and append standard error streams to the model context window.

docs.title: Reasoning & Cognitive Loops
docs.text: This guide outlines reasoning and cognitive loop templates utilized within Rahul-Chaube-Skills (RCS) to enhance problem-solving accuracy.

docs.title: 🧠 Chain-of-Thought (CoT) and Reflection
docs.text:
When faced with a complex task or an unexpected error, the agent must not guess the solution. It must run through an internal reasoning loop.

graph TD
    Trigger[Problem / Error] --> CoT[1. Chain-of-Thought: List facts & rules]
    CoT --> Hypothesize[2. Hypothesize: Formulate 2-3 possible causes]
    Hypothesize --> TestHyp[3. Test: Validate in isolation]
    TestHyp -- Success --> Implement[4. Implement solution]
    TestHyp -- Failure --> Reflect[5. Reflect: What assumption was wrong?]
    Reflect --> CoT

docs.title: 🛠️ Self-Correction Framework
docs.text:
If a change results in a compile error or test failure, apply the Self-Correction Framework:

  1. Observe: Read the exact compile error message. Do not assume you know where the error is.
  2. Locate: Look up the exact file and line number mentioned in the error. View the file lines using view_file to inspect the code context.
  3. Verify the AST: Check for missing brackets, semicolons, quotes, or misplaced indentation.
  4. Iterative Refinement: Correct the error surgically. Run the compiler/tests again. Do not guess and check in a loop—if the first correction fails, stop and perform a full review of the code changes.

docs.title: Research Methodology
docs.text: This document outlines the research standards required for agents using the Rahul-Chaube-Skills (RCS) library to explore codebases, investigate bugs, and gather requirements.

docs.title: 🔍 Codebase Exploration
docs.text:
When starting a task in an unfamiliar codebase, the agent must proceed systematically:

graph TD
    Start[Explore Task] --> ListDir[1. List directory structure]
    ListDir --> GrepKey[2. Grep search key terms & symbols]
    GrepKey --> ViewDocs[3. View configuration & README files]
    ViewDocs --> TraceFlow[4. Trace execution flow in code files]
    TraceFlow --> Formulate[5. Formulate plan]

docs.title: 📝 Rules for Research
docs.text:
1. Breadth-First Search: Before reading 800 lines of a single code file, list the folder directory using list_dir or perform a global query using grep_search to understand where things are.
  2. Read Config Files First: Look for package.json, requirements.txt, tsconfig.json, or .env files. These reveal dependencies, build scripts, and library choices.
  3. Trace Symbol References: When inspecting a class or database schema, trace its references through the system to see how changes will affect other modules (collateral damage assessment).
  4. No Guesses on APIs: If using a library or tool API (e.g. AWS, Postgres, Next.js), do not guess the method signatures. Search the local documentation, view the library code, or read public documentation files if available.

docs.title: Tool Utilization & Safety
docs.text: This document guides AI agents on safe, efficient, and aligned tool execution inside the Rahul-Chaube-Skills (RCS) framework.

docs.title: 🛠️ Tool Calling Rules
docs.text:
1. Parameters First: Always specify arguments explicitly. Do not leave optional parameters to default if specific behavior is required (e.g. Overwrite: true vs. false).
  2. Path Resolution: Use absolute paths for all file system tools (view_file, write_to_file, replace_file_content). Use forward slashes / for Windows compatibility.
  3. Command Safety:
  4. Do NOT propose commands that perform broad deletions without confirmation.
  5. Do NOT run arbitrary scripts downloaded from the web without review.
  6. Avoid executing endless polling commands (e.g. tail -f). Use background tasks or wait for completion.

docs.title: 🛑 Tool Error Recovery
docs.text:
If a tool call fails, follow this recovery workflow:

graph TD
    ToolFail[Tool Error] --> ReadError[1. Read error message carefully]
    ReadError --> CheckArgs[2. Check parameter types & path existence]
    CheckArgs -- Invalid Path --> CreateDir[3a. Create parent directories]
    CheckArgs -- Permission Denied --> RequestPerm[3b. Call ask_permission tool]
    CheckArgs -- CLI Command Failed --> RunIsolation[3c. Test simplified command]
    CreateDir --> Retry[4. Retry original tool call]
    RequestPerm --> Retry
    RunIsolation --> Retry

docs.title: Vision & Multimodal Execution
docs.text: This guide covers instructions for multimodal AI agents executing visual verification and UI/GUI interactions.

docs.title: 👁️ Visual Inspection & Verification
docs.text: Multimodal models can inspect layout outputs, diagrams, screenshots, and visual designs to ensure quality.

docs.title: Rules for Vision Tasks:
docs.text:
1. Take Screenshots: When writing frontend UI code (React, HTML/CSS), run the dev server and take screenshots using rendering tools or browser devtools MCP (take_screenshot).
  2. Review Layout Alignment:
  3. Verify that elements do not overlap.
  4. Ensure color palettes match the design systems.
  5. Check text wrapping and padding consistency across desktop/mobile views.
  6. OCR Processing: When analyzing scanned documents, logs, or command-line screenshots:
  7. Identify text coordinates carefully.
  8. Extract error logs from terminal frames.
  9. Avoid guessing text that is blurry or low resolution.

docs.title: 🎨 UI/UX Design Verification
docs.text:
When creating or modifying mockups/designs:

  • Verify that user flows (e.g. login -> dashboard) are intuitive and do not lead to dead ends.
  • Use carousels and slideshows inside walkthroughs to present UI evolution (before vs. after).
  • Avoid generic styling. Leverage cohesive theme configurations (e.g. glassmorphism, tailored CSS vars).

docs.title: Workflows & Task Execution
docs.text: This document details workflow design patterns for complex software development tasks using Rahul-Chaube-Skills (RCS).

docs.title: 🗂️ Checklists and Task Tracking (task.md)
docs.text: For any multi-step task, the agent must create and maintain a task.md file in the conversation artifacts directory.

docs.title: Checklist State Indicators:
docs.text:
• [ ] Unstarted: Task is defined but no work has begun.
  • [/] In Progress: Work is actively being performed on this item.
  • [x] Completed: Task is finished and verified.

docs.title: 🔀 Workflow Routing
docs.text:
When executing tasks:

  1. Linear Workflows: Follow steps sequentially (dependencies first). Do not skip steps or try to work on Step 3 before Step 2 is fully verified.
  2. Branching Workflows: If a step requires an architectural choice, stop and document the choices in implementation_plan.md. Wait for the user to review the plan and approve the branch.
  3. Rollbacks: If a branch fails (e.g. library dependency issue, performance failure), revert commits surgically, update task.md with the new plan, and proceed down the alternative branch.

docs.title: EverestQ AI Model Integration Guide
docs.text: This guide details the integration parameters, prompting standards, and cognitive workflows for EverestQ, supported by the Rahul-Chaube-Skills (RCS) platform.

docs.title: 🌟 Overview & Key Features
docs.text:
EverestQ is a state-of-the-art cognitive model optimized for:

  • Complex Multi-Step Logic: Native search-tree branching and backtracking.
  • Surgical Code Generation: Low AST mutation drift and high syntactic accuracy.
  • Systemic Prompt Alignment: Strict adherence to negative constraints and system XML blocks.
  • Large-scale Context Recall: Ultra-fast PagedAttention performance under large token loads.

docs.title: 💾 Context Engineering
docs.text:
EverestQ uses a dynamic context window parsing engine:

  1. XML Tag Anchoring: Separate system parameters using strict XML enclosures: 
    <system_directives>
  - Do NOT write speculative imports.
</system_directives>
  2. Context Compression: EverestQ compresses prompt spaces natively. Minimize filler tokens to decrease inference latencies.
  3. KV Cache Preservation: Cache static system instructions in persistent serving nodes to optimize throughput.

docs.title: 🎯 Prompting Best Practices
docs.text:
• Negative Constraints: State forbidden patterns clearly in uppercase or separate blocks.
  • Declarative Goals: Always state what the output should verify rather than telling it how to code.
  • Thinking Enclosures: Direct EverestQ to output its internal plans inside <thinking> blocks before writing any outputs.

docs.title: 🛠️ Tool Calling & Agent Workflows
docs.text:
EverestQ supports high-fidelity JSON function schemas:

  • Input Sanitization: Always validate tool arguments against strict Pydantic schemas.
  • Graceful Handshake: EverestQ detects tool execution failures and retries dynamically using self-correction logic.
# EverestQ Tool Integration Template
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

class FileModifySchema(BaseModel):
    filepath: str = Field(..., description="Absolute path of file to modify")
    target_text: str = Field(..., description="Precise target text to replace")
    replacement: str = Field(..., description="Replacement drop-in content")

docs.title: 💻 Coding & Deep Research
docs.text:
• Surgical Precision: EverestQ excels at line-targeted modifications.
  • Deep Research Planner: Integrates with search execution chains, formulating specific query expansions and checking citation URLs for integrity.

docs.title: Python API Integration
docs.text:
import openai

client = openai.OpenAI(
    base_url="https://api.everestq.ai/v1",
    api_key="your_everestq_api_key"
)

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="everestq-large",
    messages=[
        {"role": "system", "content": "<rules>Follow RCS surgical coding standards</rules>"},
        {"role": "user", "content": "Fix typo in line 45 of utils.py."}
    ],
    temperature=0.0
)
print(response.choices[0].message.content)

docs.title: 🚀 Production Recommendations
docs.text:
• Temperature Setting: Use 0.0 for code-writing, debugging, and research tasks; use 0.5 for document summaries.
  • Serving Engines: Deploy EverestQ on vLLM clusters using PagedAttention configurations to maximize parallel execution throughput.

docs.title: Learning Pathways Overview
docs.text: Welcome to the RCS Learning Pathways. These guides are designed to take you from a developer writing your first API request to an expert optimizing large-scale serving clusters and distributed training graphs.

docs.title: 🗺️ Choose Your Path
docs.text:
Select a pathway based on your experience and career objectives:

Pathway Target Audience Key Concepts Beginner AI Engineer Software developers new to AI integration. Prompt variables, APIs, JSON Outputs, local models. Intermediate Agent Developer Developers building interactive flows. ReAct loops, planning, tools integrations, state. Advanced Cognitive Architect Systems engineers designing autonomous nodes. GraphRAG, Tree of Thoughts, context engineering. Expert Systems Optimizer Infrastructure architects scaling models. Serving (vLLM), KV cache, quantization, distributed.

docs.title: 📅 Structured Curriculums
docs.text:
If you prefer a timeline-guided roadmap, explore our unified Curriculums:

  • 30-Day Learning Plan: Build your first custom coding assistant.
  • 90-Day Mastery Roadmap: Design and deploy multi-agent RAG pipelines.
  • 365-Day AI Engineer Curriculum: Research and train custom LoRAs and optimize execution runtimes.

docs.title: Pathway: Advanced Cognitive Architect
docs.text: This pathway covers advanced reasoning (Tree of Thoughts, Graph of Thoughts), context compression, knowledge graphs, and hybrid retrieval.

docs.title: 1. Advanced Reasoning Structures
docs.text:
• Tree of Thoughts (ToT): Branching candidate solutions and evaluating them via a search algorithm (DFS/BFS).
  • Self-Consistency: Generating multiple reasoning paths and selecting the majority response.

docs.title: 2. Context Engineering & Compression
docs.text:
• Prompt Compression: Removing redundant text while preserving semantic context.
  • Episodic vs Semantic Memory: Separating past user experiences from general factual indexing.

docs.title: 3. GraphRAG & Hybrid Search
docs.text:
• Generating entities and relations from text documents.
  • Linking vector indexes with Neo4j knowledge graphs to support multi-hop reasoning.

docs.title: 🛠️ Step-by-Step Exercise
docs.text:
1. Step 1: Write a script to build a small entity-relation graph in Neo4j.
  2. Step 2: Create a hybrid retriever combining vector search and Cypher graph queries.
  3. Step 3: Compare search accuracy against standard vector-only retrieval.

docs.title: Pathway: Beginner AI Engineer
docs.text: This pathway introduces you to prompt design, APIs, structured outputs, and basic model configurations.

docs.title: 1. Prompt Anatomy & System Rules
docs.text:
Learn how to structure a prompt so that the LLM behaves predictably:

  • System Prompts: Enforce global constraints (e.g. "Do not write explanations").
  • User Prompt: The direct task query.
  • Enclosures: Use XML tags to isolate variables (e.g. <code_to_fix>{{code}}</code_to_fix>).

docs.title: 2. JSON Schema & Structured Outputs
docs.text:
Ensure the model returns raw JSON data instead of text paragraphs.

  • Using OpenAI's response_format: {"type": "json_object"}.
  • Writing Pydantic schemas to validate outputs in Python.

docs.title: 3. Model APIs and Tool Definitions
docs.text:
• Making your first client requests to EverestQ, OpenAI, Anthropic, or local model endpoints.
  • Defining a basic JSON tool schema to let the model request math calculations or file readings.

docs.title: 🛠️ Step-by-Step Exercise
docs.text:
1. Step 1: Write a Python script to request a structured translation from an LLM.
  2. Step 2: Use Pydantic to validate that the output contains translated_text and confidence_score.
  3. Step 3: Handle validation errors gracefully.

docs.title: AI Engineer Curriculums
docs.text: This document outlines structural curricula to help you master AI engineering across different timelines.

docs.title: 30-Day Learning Plan: Agent Fundamentals
docs.text:
• Goal: Build and deploy a single-agent terminal developer tool with tool integrations.
  • Timeline:
  • Days 1–7: Fundamentals of structured prompts and JSON outputs.
  • Days 8–14: Tool calling and function schemas.
  • Days 15–22: Building a basic ReAct loop in Python.
  • Days 23–30: Final Project - Coding assistant that formats and runs lint tests on git changes.

docs.title: 90-Day Mastery Roadmap: Production Cognitive Systems
docs.text:
• Goal: Build and evaluate multi-agent reasoning graphs with GraphRAG.
  • Timeline:
  • Days 1–30: Master Agent frameworks (LangGraph, CrewAI). Build planning, routing, and memory loops.
  • Days 31–60: Knowledge Retrieval and Hybrid Search (Neo4j, Vector Databases, Chunking).
  • Days 61–90: Final Project - Create an Enterprise Knowledge base that answers queries by traversing knowledge graphs and vectors.

docs.title: 365-Day AI Engineer Curriculum: Systems & Research
docs.text:
• Goal: Optimize model serve latencies, fine-tune adapters, and compile custom pipelines.
  • Timeline:
  • Months 1–3: Deepening agent architectures, memory compactions, and advanced evaluations.
  • Months 4–6: Local deployment (vLLM, Ollama), GPU memory metrics, and PagedAttention.
  • Months 7–9: LoRA, QLoRA fine-tuning, synthetic data generation, and DPO alignments.
  • Months 10–12: Distributed setups (DeepSpeed, Megatron), CUDA optimization, and hosting serving nodes.

docs.title: Pathway: Expert Systems Optimizer
docs.text: This pathway covers model serving engines (vLLM, TensorRT-LLM), memory pooling (PagedAttention), quantization, and distributed scaling patterns.

docs.title: 1. High-Performance Serving
docs.text:
• vLLM: Serves models with extreme speed by optimizing KV Cache allocation.
  • PagedAttention: Prevents GPU memory fragmentation by dividing KV cache into pages (similar to virtual memory in operating systems).

docs.title: 2. Model Quantization
docs.text:
• Converting FP16 weights to INT8 or INT4 to fit large models on smaller GPU footprints.
  • Understanding AWQ, GPTQ, and GGUF quantization formats.

docs.title: 3. Distributed Infrastructure
docs.text:
• Setting up tensor parallelism and pipeline parallelism.
  • Configuring DeepSpeed or Ray clusters to distribute weights across multiple GPUs.

docs.title: 🛠️ Step-by-Step Exercise
docs.text:
1. Step 1: Spin up a vLLM server locally or on a cloud instance.
  2. Step 2: Profile server throughput (tokens per second) under varying request volumes.
  3. Step 3: Configure AWQ quantization and measure the reduction in GPU memory utilization.

docs.title: Pathway: Intermediate Agent Developer
docs.text: This pathway covers building interactive agents, ReAct loops, planning nodes, state tracking, and tool integrations.

docs.title: 1. ReAct (Reason-Action) Loop
docs.text:
Understand the classic agentic loop: Thought -> Action -> Observation -> Thought...

  • How to write a manual loop in Python that executes model-selected tools.
  • Preventing infinite execution loops when tools return errors.

docs.title: 2. State & Memory Tracking
docs.text:
• Staging conversation history dynamically.
  • Implementing databases (like SQLite or Redis) to save agent state.

docs.title: 3. Agent Frameworks (LangGraph, CrewAI)
docs.text:
• Building branching logic graphs.
  • Setting up router nodes that guide conversations to specialized agents (e.g. Code Agent vs. Search Agent).

docs.title: 🛠️ Step-by-Step Exercise
docs.text:
1. Step 1: Build a ReAct agent using LangGraph.
  2. Step 2: Add a search tool and a file-write tool.
  3. Step 3: Verify that the agent can search for information, write it to a markdown file, and successfully end its turn.

docs.title: Reference Project Blueprints
docs.text: This directory contains full architectural and implementation blueprints for five production-grade AI agents and systems.

docs.title: 🚀 Reference Catalog
docs.text: Project Description Architecture Highlights Coding Agent Autonomous local coder. Linter feedback loops, git stage checks, AST verification. Deep Research Agent Literature review synthesizer. Parallel web searches, source verification, citation tagging. Browser Agent Vision-guided computer operator. Page screenshots, coordinate grids, DOM mapping. GraphRAG Assistant Neo4j + Vector search engine. Hybrid routing, entity mapping, contextual merge. Voice Agent Ultra-low latency voice companion. STT / TTS streams, sentence buffering, interrupt handling.

docs.title: 🛠️ Design Standards
docs.text:
Every project blueprint implements:

  • Architectural Diagrams: Mermaid blocks detailing communication flows.
  • Folder Structures: Clear organization of files.
  • Best Practices: Performance tuning and cost bounds.
  • Security Checklists: Secrets, inputs validation, and sandboxing rules.

docs.title: Project Blueprint: Vision-Guided Browser Agent
docs.text: This document details the layout and architecture of a computer use agent navigating web pages using screenshot processing and element coordinates.

docs.title: 🏗️ System Architecture
docs.text:
graph TD
    Goal[User Task: Buy a ticket] --> AgentCore[Agent Core Node]
    AgentCore --> TakeScreenshot[1. Take Page Screenshot]
    TakeScreenshot --> ElementMap[2. Map interactive DOM elements]
    ElementMap --> OverlayGrid[3. Draw visual coordinate grid on image]
    OverlayGrid --> MultimodalLLM[4. Feed Gridded Image to Vision LLM]
    MultimodalLLM --> ChooseAction[5. Select Action: Click, Type, Scroll]
    ChooseAction --> ClickNode[6. Execute Click on coordinates (X, Y)]
    ClickNode --> TakeScreenshot

docs.title: 🗂️ Project Directory Layout
docs.text:
browser-agent/
├── src/
│   ├── browser/
│   │   ├── driver.py        # Playwright controller (navigate, screenshot)
│   │   └── dom.py           # Extract clickable elements and input selectors
│   ├── vision/
│   │   └── grid.py          # Draws visual overlays and numbers on image frames
│   ├── core/
│   │   └── loop.py          # Action parsing loop (click, scroll, keystroke)
│   └── main.py
├── requirements.txt
└── README.md

docs.title: 💡 Best Practices & Safety
docs.text:
1. Relative Coordinates: Scale screen coordinates (0-1000) to actual browser viewport widths dynamically to prevent misclicks on different display sizes.
  2. Action Delay: Introduce randomized human-like delays (500ms - 1500ms) between clicks to prevent server-side blockages.
  3. Transaction Safety: Implement blocklists for domains. Never let the agent execute actions on checkouts or payment fields unless running in sandbox test modes.
  4. Retry Loop Limits: Cap page action retries at 5. If the page does not change state, stop and report the blockade to the user.

docs.title: Project Blueprint: Autonomous Coding Agent
docs.text: This document details the architecture and implementation specifications for an autonomous local software engineering agent.

docs.title: 🏗️ System Architecture
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant Agent Core
    participant Git Helper
    participant Compiler / Linter
    participant Workspace

    User->>Agent Core: Request Feature / Fix
    Agent Core->>Workspace: Inspect files (view_file)
    Agent Core->>Workspace: Write implementation plan
    Agent Core->>Git Helper: Save starting git checkpoint
    Agent Core->>Workspace: Edit target files (replace_file_content)
    Agent Core->>Compiler / Linter: Execute build & tests (npx eslint)
    alt Linter Fail
        Compiler / Linter-->>Agent Core: Parse compilation error logs
        Agent Core->>Workspace: Surgically fix AST errors
    else Linter Pass
        Compiler / Linter-->>Agent Core: Report 0 errors
    end
    Agent Core->>Git Helper: Stage & Commit changes
    Agent Core->>User: walkthrough.md summary & diff

docs.title: 🗂️ Project Directory Layout
docs.text:
coding-agent/
├── src/
│   ├── core/
│   │   ├── agent.py         # Main agent loop (planning -> execute -> evaluate)
│   │   └── memory.py        # Token compression & transcript logging
│   ├── tools/
│   │   ├── git.py           # Git checkout, diff, and status bindings
│   │   ├── file.py          # Surgical file replace & read tools
│   │   └── shell.py         # Sanatized shell execution node
│   └── main.py              # CLI Entry point
├── tests/
│   └── test_agent.py        # System mock integration tests
├── requirements.txt
└── README.md

docs.title: 💡 Best Practices & Security
docs.text:
1. Sandboxing: Never execute shell commands directly on the host system in production. Use dockerized environments with strict resource constraints.
  2. Checkpointing: Always run git stash or create a temporary git branch (agent/work-xxxx) before letting the agent modify files. This guarantees easy rollbacks.
  3. AST Validation: Parse modified Python files using the ast module or load TypeScript files into compiler APIs locally to verify syntactical correctness before running any test command.

docs.title: Project Blueprint: Deep Research Agent
docs.text: A structured literature-review and research agent capable of parallel search query execution, source verification, and citation synthesis.

docs.title: 🏗️ System Architecture
docs.text:
graph TD
    Query[User Research Query] --> Planner[Research Planner: Break down topics]
    Planner --> SearchExecutor[Search Executor: Parallel web searches]
    SearchExecutor --> Scraper[Scraper Node: Extract text from pages]
    Scraper --> FactCheck[Fact Verification Node: Cross-examine sources]
    FactCheck --> Synthesizer[Synthesizer: Compile PDF paper]
    Synthesizer --> CiteChecker[Citation Checker: Validate URL references]
    CiteChecker -- Missing Cite --> Synthesizer
    CiteChecker -- Validated --> Output[Final Research Paper]

docs.title: 🗂️ Project Directory Layout
docs.text:
research-agent/
├── src/
│   ├── planner.py           # Generates search sub-queries
│   ├── search.py            # Interfaces Google/Bing/Tavily search APIs
│   ├── parser.py            # Converts raw HTML/PDFs into clean markdown
│   ├── verifier.py          # LLM fact-checking consensus node
│   └── compiler.py          # Generates formatted PDF/LaTeX output
├── tests/
│   └── test_verifier.py
├── requirements.txt
└── README.md

docs.title: 💡 Best Practices & Scaling
docs.text:
1. Query Expansion: Expand the user's brief query into 5-10 specific queries (e.g. searching for specific academic names, dates, or database metrics).
  2. Context Deduplication: Run text similarity checks (e.g. Cosine Similarity) on scrapped text blocks before feeding them to the model context. This avoids wasting tokens on duplicate web page structures.
  3. Citation Anchoring: Use regex verification to ensure that every citation number (e.g. [1]) matches a valid, resolving link in the bibliography page.

docs.title: Project Blueprint: GraphRAG Assistant
docs.text: A structured knowledge retriever combining vector similarities and Neo4j relational graph Cypher queries.

docs.title: 🏗️ System Architecture
docs.text:
graph TD
    UserQuery[User Query] --> Router{Categorize Query}
    Router -- Structural / Relation --> GraphQuery[Neo4j Cypher Query]
    Router -- Semantic / Textual --> VectorQuery[Vector Search: Qdrant]
    Router -- Hybrid --> JointQuery[Parallel Graph & Vector retrieval]
    GraphQuery --> CypherExec[Run Cypher matching]
    VectorQuery --> SimilarityMatch[Vector embedding match]
    CypherExec --> ContextFusion[Context Fusion & Re-ranking]
    SimilarityMatch --> ContextFusion
    ContextFusion --> AnswerGen[LLM Response Generation]
    AnswerGen --> Response[Final Answer]

docs.title: 🗂️ Project Directory Layout
docs.text:
graph-rag/
├── src/
│   ├── ingestion/
│   │   ├── parser.py        # PDF extraction & text chunker
│   │   ├── entities.py      # LLM entity-relationship extractor
│   │   └── graph_writer.py  # Populates Neo4j nodes and edges
│   ├── retrieval/
│   │   ├── vector_search.py # Queries Qdrant vector index
│   │   ├── cypher_gen.py    # Translates query to Neo4j Cypher matches
│   │   └── fusion.py        # Combines graph maps and vector texts
│   └── main.py
├── requirements.txt
└── README.md

docs.title: 💡 Best Practices & Scaling
docs.text:
1. Entity Normalization: Ensure that entities like "OpenAI" and "OpenAI Inc." are merged into a single graph node during extraction to prevent graph fragmentation.
  2. Chunking Bounds: Store chunk text inside vector databases, but link chunks to their source parent nodes in the Neo4j graph. This allows the retriever to pull surrounding context easily.
  3. Query Sanitization: Never inject user query strings directly into Cypher statements. Parse queries to parameters or validate generated Cypher statements using a safe schema parser.

docs.title: Project Blueprint: Real-Time Voice Agent
docs.text: This document details the architecture of an ultra-low latency voice assistant integrating STT (Speech-to-Text) streaming, dynamic LLM prompt generation, and TTS (Text-to-Speech) chunk buffering.

docs.title: 🏗️ System Architecture
docs.text:
graph LR
    UserMic[User Microphone] -->|PCM Audio Stream| STT[Whisper Streaming Server]
    STT -->|Transcribed Text| SessionCoordinator[Session Coordinator: Websockets]
    SessionCoordinator -->|Prompt Context| LLM[LLM Response Stream]
    LLM -->|Token Streams| TTS[TTS Engine: ElevenLabs / Cartesia]
    TTS -->|Audio Chunk Buffer| Speaker[User Speaker]

    Speaker -.->|User Interrupt Detection| SessionCoordinator
    SessionCoordinator -.->|Halt current queue| TTS

docs.title: 🗂️ Project Directory Layout
docs.text:
voice-agent/
├── src/
│   ├── audio/
│   │   ├── recorder.py      # Captures local PCM microphone inputs
│   │   └── player.py        # Plays back audio chunk buffers
│   ├── services/
│   │   ├── whisper.py       # Interfaces WebSocket STT stream
│   │   ├── cartesia.py      # Interfaces WebSocket TTS stream
│   │   └── llm.py           # Orchestrates chat model response streaming
│   ├── server.py            # Main WebSocket server entry
│   └── coordinator.py       # Handles interrupts and queue resets
├── requirements.txt
└── README.md

docs.title: 💡 Latency Optimization & Interrupts
docs.text:
1. Streaming Connections: Keep persistent WebSockets open for both STT and TTS services. Avoid overhead from making REST HTTP requests.
  2. Interrupt Handling: Monitor the microphone stream for active user speech while the speaker is playing. If voice activity (VAD) is detected, immediately send a clear_buffer socket signal to the TTS player and stop LLM generation.
  3. Sentence Buffering: Feed LLM output tokens to the TTS in small sentence chunks (delimited by ., ?, or ,) rather than waiting for full paragraphs. This drops latency from ~2s to < 200ms.
  4. VAD Settings: Use a lightweight Voice Activity Detector (like Silero VAD) locally in python to detect speech starts in real time.

docs.title: Skills Library Catalog
docs.text: Welcome to the RCS Skills Ecosystem Index. This directory holds 500+ modular instructions categorized into 10 key domains:

docs.title: Agentic Cognition
docs.text:
• React: Reasoning and acting loops for dynamic tool invocation.
  • Tree Of Thoughts: Branching search tree for candidate solutions and evaluations.
  • Graph Of Thoughts: Network-structured thought blocks showing loops and merges.
  • Self Consistency: Decoding multiple paths and selecting response by voting.
  • Chain Of Thought: Enforcing step-by-step logic detailing assumptions.
  • Plan And Solve: Dividing tasks into steps and solving sequentially.
  • Reflection Loop: Self-evaluation loop of outputs against requirements.
  • Self Correction: Reading compilation errors and repairing AST surgically.
  • Backtracking: Identifying logical dead-ends and reversing steps.
  • Consensual Voting: Polling multiple internal models to decide actions.
  • Critic Model: Acting as a dedicated evaluator of primary output quality.
  • Recursive Refinement: Iterative polishing of code or text to meet criteria.
  • Mental Simulation: Simulating execution steps before running shell commands.
  • Hypothesis Testing: Isolating code paths to confirm bugs before edits.
  • Subgoal Querying: Asking the user about ambiguous paths early in the plan.

docs.title: Context Engineering
docs.text:
• Prompt Compression: Removing redundant tokens while preserving semantic intent.
  • Context Pruning: Scanning files and loading only relevant line boundaries.
  • Episodic Memory: Storing past user conversation states in a database.
  • Semantic Memory: Vector embedding searches to retrieve factual data.
  • Context Compaction: Summarizing older history to save context-window bounds.
  • Token Budgeting: Calculating prompt allocations dynamically to prevent overflows.
  • Needle Retrieval: Optimizing context placement of critical variables.
  • System Prompts: Tuning instruction bounds at the bottom of contexts.
  • Xml Tagging: Enclosing input variables and schemas in XML labels.
  • Negative Constraints: Writing capital negative boundaries to override defaults.
  • Knowledge Distillation: Compiling massive logs into lightweight rule files.
  • Metadata Filtering: Pre-filtering retrievals to conserve search context.
  • Hierarchical Indexing: Retrieving outline maps before pulling raw text.
  • Context Caching: Leveraging serve cache tags to reduce latency fees.
  • Dynamic Context: Injecting tool outputs conditionally based on query size.

docs.title: Model Serving
docs.text:
• Vllm Serving: Configuring vLLM servers for fast throughput.
  • Paged Attention: Optimizing KV cache allocations to prevent GPU fragmentation.
  • Tensor Parallelism: Distributing weights across multiple GPUs in a node.
  • Pipeline Parallelism: Dividing model layers across distinct GPU nodes.
  • Kv Cache Tuning: Setting cache block allocations based on request volume.
  • Sglang Serving: Executing high-speed structured serving routines.
  • Quantization Awq: Quantizing models to INT4 using Activation-aware Weight Quantization.
  • Quantization Gptq: Compiling INT4/INT8 models with GPTQ algorithms.
  • Gguf Compilation: Compiling GGUF assets for CPU/local deployment.
  • Speculative Decoding: Using draft models to speed up target model output generations.
  • Triton Server: Setting up Triton servers for multi-framework hosting.
  • Deepspeed Optimization: Optimizing model memory states using DeepSpeed ZeRO.
  • Ray Distributed: Scaling agent and model runs across Ray clusters.
  • Vllm Concurrency: Tuning request concurrency and queue parameters.
  • Continuous Batching: Grouping dynamic queries inside GPU execution batches.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Network-structured thought blocks showing loops and merges. This deep-dive module provides advanced prompt engineering and systemic context rules.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
• When building production-ready cognitive loops requiring high logical alignment.
  • When standard models drift or ignore simple constraints.

docs.title: When NOT to Use
docs.text: • For simple one-off tasks where speed and low latency are prioritized.

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• Define Explicit Goals: Structure success boundaries before generating inputs.
  • Negative Enclosures: Wrap forbidden patterns inside <not_allowed> tags.
  • AST Checks: Validate code formatting prior to test execution.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Premature Execution: Letting the model edit files without creating an implementation plan.
  • Over-Abstraction: Creating abstract interfaces where single functions would suffice.

docs.title: Advanced Techniques
docs.text:
• Dynamic Context Injection: Injecting variables dynamically based on prompt length constraints.
  • Self-Healing AST: Interfacing compiler stack traces directly to model correction prompts.

docs.title: Prompt Template
docs.text:
<instructions>
You are executing the GRAPH-OF-THOUGHTS skill. 
Analyze the task below, write an implementation plan, and verify all constraints.
</instructions>
<task>
{{task_description}}
</task>

docs.title: Production Use Case
docs.text: • Automated local refactoring pipelines correcting linter errors autonomously in CI/CD cycles.

docs.title: Evaluation Checklist
docs.text:
• [ ] Model states assumptions explicitly.
  • [ ] No speculative helper functions are written.
  • [ ] Output formatting passes lint validation check.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Reasoning and acting loops for dynamic tool invocation. This deep-dive module provides advanced prompt engineering and systemic context rules.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
• When building production-ready cognitive loops requiring high logical alignment.
  • When standard models drift or ignore simple constraints.

docs.title: When NOT to Use
docs.text: • For simple one-off tasks where speed and low latency are prioritized.

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• Define Explicit Goals: Structure success boundaries before generating inputs.
  • Negative Enclosures: Wrap forbidden patterns inside <not_allowed> tags.
  • AST Checks: Validate code formatting prior to test execution.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Premature Execution: Letting the model edit files without creating an implementation plan.
  • Over-Abstraction: Creating abstract interfaces where single functions would suffice.

docs.title: Advanced Techniques
docs.text:
• Dynamic Context Injection: Injecting variables dynamically based on prompt length constraints.
  • Self-Healing AST: Interfacing compiler stack traces directly to model correction prompts.
